content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 14, 1990


Leo B. Newton
Attorney at Law
8130 East Florence Avenue
Downey, CA 90240

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-113

Dear Mr. Newton:

This is in response to your request for advice concerning the
contribution limitations of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. You are the treasurer and sole member of a non-controlled
committee that contributes to judicial candidates. Can the
committee make contributions to a judicial candidate who has asked
you to be the treasurer of his campaign committee?

2. If the answer to Question 1 is yes, is there a limit on
the amount your committee can contribute to the judicial
candidate?

3. If your committee makes a contribution to the judicial
candidate, does it make a difference as to whether you can serve
as treasurer for that candidate's campaign committee?

CONCLUSION

1. The fact that a candidate has asked you to serve as
treasurer for his campaign committee does not preclude you from
making a contribution to that candidate from your own non-
candidate controlled committee.

_________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-90-113
Page 2


     2.  There is a limit on how much your committee may
contribute to a candidate during a fiscal year.  From the facts
presented, it appears that your committee may contribute a maximum
of $1,000 to a candidate during a fiscal year.

     3.  You may act as treasurer for the judicial candidate's
campaign committee whether or not your committee has contributed
to that candidate.

                              FACTS

     You are treasurer and sole member of a committee identified
as "Committee for the Independence of Downey Court."  This
committee is not a candidate-controlled committee but exists to,
among other things, make contributions to candidates for judicial
office.

     You have recently been asked by a judicial candidate to act
as the treasurer for his campaign committee.

                              ANALYSIS

     Sections 85100 through 85307 set forth the only limitations
in the Act on making contributions to candidates for elective
office.  These limitations apply equally to candidates for
election to judicial and non-judicial office.2

     While these provisions place limitations on the amounts that
can be contributed (see Sections 85301 through 85303 and 85305)
and the use of contributions (see Sections 85200, 85202 and
85304),3 neither these nor any other provisions of the Act
prohibit a person from simultaneously running a political action
committee and serving as treasurer for a candidate to whom the
committee makes or may make contributions.  Therefore, the Act
does not prevent you from making contributions to the judicial
candidate on behalf of your committee and simultaneously serving
as treasurer for that candidate's campaign committee.


_____________________________
2    Sections 85100 through 85307 make repeated reference to
"candidates" for office.  Section 82007 defines "candidate," in
pertinent part, as "an individual who is listed on the ballot ...
for nomination for or election to any elective office, who
receives a contribution ... with a view to bringing about his or
her nomination or election to any elective office, whether or not
the specific elective office ... is known at the time the
contribution is received...."  (Emphasis added.)

3    Section 85306 was invalidated in the case of Service Employees
International Union, et al. v. Fair Political Practices
Commission, U.S. District Court, Eastern District of California,
Case No. 89-0433 LKK-JFM.

Our File No. A-90-113
Page 3


     However, the Act does impose limits upon the amount your
committee may make in contributions to the judicial candidate.
Depending on the composition of a particular committee, it may
make a maximum contribution of $1,000, $2,500 or $5,000 to a
candidate in a fiscal year.4  Since your committee is operated
solely by you and not in conjunction with one or more other
persons, it does not appear to qualify as a political committee5
or broad based political committee6 under the Act's contribution
limits. Accordingly, it is classified as a person, which is
defined in Section 85102(b) as:

     [A]n individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership,
     joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company,
     corporation, association, committee, and labor
     organization.

                                        (Emphasis added.)

     Section 85301(a) sets forth the limits on contributions from
persons to candidates, stating:

     No person shall make, and no candidate for elective
     office, or campaign treasurer, shall solicit or
     accept any contribution or loan which would cause
     the total amount contributed or loaned by that
     person to that candidate, including contributions
     or loans to all committees controlled by the
     candidate, to exceed one thousand dollars ($1,000)
     in any fiscal year.

     Therefore, if your committee chooses to make a contribution to the
judicial candidate, or any other candidate for elective office, it may
only give that candidate a maximum of $1,000 in a fiscal year.

     Finally, please note that, for the purpose of calculating the
$1,000 contribution limit, any contribution made to a candidate by
your committee will be cumulated with any contributions you make
to the same candidate from your own personal funds.  In its

_____________
4    Section 85102(a) defines "fiscal year" as the period from
July 1 through June 30.

5    Section 85102(c) defines "political committee" as "a committee
of persons who receive contributions from two or more persons and
acting in concert makes contributions to candidates."  (Emphasis
added.)

6    Section 85102(d) defines "broad based political committee" as
"a committee of persons which has been in existence for more than
six months, receives contributions from one hundred or more
persons, and acting in concert makes contributions to five or more
candidates."  (Emphasis added.)

Our File No. A-90-113
Page 4

opinion entitled In re LUMSDON (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 140 (copy
enclosed), the Commission concluded that cumulation was required
where separate contributions were made by a corporation and the
corporation's majority shareholder. The rationale for this
conclusion was that one person was in fact controlling both his
personal contributions and those of the corporation.

Subsequent decisions by the Commission and its staff have
required cumulation of contributions where the factual
circumstances have slightly differed but the general rationale was
still relevant. (See In re Kahn (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 150
(contributions by parent company and wholly owned subsidiary);
Regulation 18531.5 (contributions by two or more entities where
the same person in fact directs and controls the entities'
contribution decisions); Leigh Advice Letter, No. 89-637
(contributions by a law firm and its partners), copies enclosed.)
The underlying rationale for these decisions is present in your
situation. You alone control both your personal contributions and
those of your committee. Accordingly, any contributions made by
your committee must be cumulated with any personal contributions
you may make to the same candidate.

Should you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Scott Hallabrin
    Counsel, Legal Division

KED:SH:ld
